questions.question_text: When must a Connecticut agent first disclose their agency status to another party?
questions.correct_answer: At the beginning of the first personal meeting
distractors: At the time a written offer is first presented
Only when a purchase contract is signed
Within 48 hours after showing the property
questions.explanation: Connecticut requires agency disclosure at the beginning of the first personal meeting with any party. This ensures all parties understand the agent's role from the beginning of any substantive interaction.

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent meets a seller at an open house for the first time. What must the agent do regarding agency disclosure?
questions.correct_answer: Disclose their buyer's agent status to the seller
distractors: Wait until presenting an offer to disclose
Only disclose if the seller asks directly
Disclosure is optional at open houses
questions.explanation: Agency disclosure must happen at the beginning of the first personal meeting, which includes meeting at an open house. The agent must inform the seller that they represent the buyer.

questions.question_text: Once signed, the written agency disclosure given to an unrepresented buyer must be:
questions.correct_answer: Attached to any offer or binder that follows
distractors: Filed with the Department of Consumer Protection
Recorded with the town clerk before closing
Notarized and retained only by the brokerage
questions.explanation: Reg. 20-325d-5(a): the disclosure is given at the beginning of the first personal meeting about the buyer's needs, signed, and attached to any offer, binder, option or purchase agreement. If the buyer refuses to sign, the agent notes the refusal on the form.

questions.question_text: The agency disclosure must explain to the consumer:
questions.correct_answer: Available agency types and the agent's role
distractors: The specific commission rate being charged
The agent's annual sales production history
The current appraised value of the property
questions.explanation: CGS 20-325d(b) requires written disclosure of the types of agency relationships available and a warning not to share confidential information before signing a representation agreement; the DCP form must clearly state who is and is not represented. Commission rates and sales history are not required.

questions.question_text: A listing agent receives a call from an unrepresented buyer interested in seeing a property. When must the written agency disclosure be given?
questions.correct_answer: When the first personal meeting begins
distractors: During the initial telephone conversation
Not until the buyer submits a written offer
Within 24 hours after the phone inquiry
questions.explanation: CGS 20-325d(b) and Reg. 20-325d-5 require written disclosure not later than the beginning of the first personal meeting concerning the buyer's specific needs. A telephone call is not a personal meeting, so the disclosure is due when they first meet — typically at the showing.

questions.question_text: In 2024, Connecticut changed how dual and designated agency forms are managed. What happened?
questions.correct_answer: Forms moved from regulations to DCP templates
distractors: Dual agency was banned entirely in the state
New mandatory state-issued forms replaced old ones
All agency forms must now be notarized
questions.explanation: DCP's 2024 template forms (including a combined dual/designated agency form) replaced forms embedded in the regulations; the amended regs now point to forms posted on DCP's website. The statutory Dual Agency Consent Agreement in CGS 20-325g and the consent requirements themselves are unchanged.

questions.question_text: A buyer wants a written buyer representation agreement. In Connecticut, this agreement:
questions.correct_answer: Is a contract between the buyer and broker
distractors: Must be approved by DCP before execution
Is only valid if countersigned by a seller
Cannot exceed 6 months in duration
questions.explanation: A buyer agency agreement is a contract between the buyer and the brokerage, establishing the terms of representation. It does not require DCP approval or seller involvement.

questions.question_text: Where can Connecticut licensees obtain the latest agency disclosure template forms?
distractors: The Connecticut General Assembly website
Local Board of Realtors offices only
They must create their own forms
questions.explanation: Since 2024, DCP provides template agency forms on its website, including the Prospective Parties Disclosure Notice and combined dual/designated agency forms. Licensees can access and use these templates.

questions.question_text: A Connecticut brokerage will represent both buyer and seller in a residential sale. Before the parties sign a purchase contract, the brokerage must obtain:
questions.correct_answer: A written dual agency consent agreement
distractors: Verbal consent from both parties at closing
Approval from the Connecticut Real Estate Commission
A notarized waiver signed by the seller only
questions.explanation: Reg. 20-325d-2(c) requires written disclosure of dual agency to all parties using the Dual Agency Consent Agreement in CGS 20-325g, which creates a conclusive presumption of informed consent when signed before any purchase or lease contract. Oral consent is insufficient.

questions.question_text: The 2024 regulatory changes to agency forms in Connecticut affected:
questions.correct_answer: Where forms are published, not the legal requirements
distractors: The types of agency permitted by law
Whether written consent is required for dual agency
The fiduciary duties owed to clients
questions.explanation: The 2024 changes moved forms from being embedded in regulations to being template forms published by DCP. The underlying legal requirements for agency disclosure and consent remain unchanged.

questions.question_text: An agent from Brokerage A contacts a buyer who has a signed buyer agency agreement with Brokerage B. This could constitute:
distractors: Normal competitive business practice
A violation of antitrust law only
Dual agency between the two brokerages
questions.explanation: Soliciting a party who has an existing agency agreement with another brokerage can constitute interference with an agency relationship, which is prohibited in Connecticut.

questions.question_text: Which of the following actions would NOT constitute interference with an existing agency relationship?
questions.correct_answer: Responding to an unsolicited inquiry from the party
distractors: Actively soliciting a listed seller to switch agents
Encouraging a buyer under contract to use your services
Contacting a seller's client to offer better terms
questions.explanation: Responding to an unsolicited inquiry is not interference — the party initiated contact. Actively soliciting someone with an existing agency agreement to switch representation would be interference.

questions.question_text: Connecticut's prohibition on interference with agency relationships protects:
questions.correct_answer: Both agents and clients from solicitation
distractors: Only the listing broker's commission income
Only buyers from undisclosed dual agency
Only sellers from receiving lowball offers
questions.explanation: The prohibition protects the integrity of existing agency relationships, benefiting both the agents who established the relationship and the clients who rely on consistent representation.

questions.question_text: A seller's listing agreement has expired. Another agent may now approach the seller because:
questions.correct_answer: No active agency relationship exists to protect
distractors: The listing agent waived their rights at expiration
A 30-day cooling period has passed
The seller publicly announced they are seeking new representation
questions.explanation: Once a listing agreement expires, there is no existing agency relationship to interfere with. Agents are free to solicit the seller for a new listing agreement.

questions.question_text: A salesperson learns that a neighbor's listing agreement expires next week. What is the appropriate action?
questions.correct_answer: Wait until the agreement expires before soliciting
distractors: Contact the seller immediately to discuss options
Ask the current listing agent for permission first
Report the listing agent to DCP for poor service
questions.explanation: While the listing agreement is active, soliciting the seller would constitute interference. The agent should wait until the agreement expires before making contact about representation.
